claimed_contribution: Shows that smartphone (vs. PC) use changes the nature of written user-generated content by increasing emotional language and reducing specificity.
Provides convergent evidence that the effect is driven by smartphone-induced brevity and a resulting focus on gist, rather than by reviewer self-selection or review timing.
Demonstrates robustness and generality across two field settings and three experiments, using multiple text-analysis tools and human judgments.
context: The paper studies online user-generated text in evaluative settings, primarily restaurant reviews and social media posts. Empirical contexts include TripAdvisor restaurant reviews (Philadelphia and San Francisco), controlled review-writing tasks, and Twitter posts about pop-culture hashtags.
design_and_data: The research includes five studies: two field studies and three controlled experiments. Study 1 analyzes 61,642 TripAdvisor restaurant reviews (47,180 PC and 14,462 smartphone) from 593 restaurants posted between 2012 and 2017, and also uses human ratings for a large subset of reviews. Studies 2-4 randomly assign participants to write reviews on smartphone versus PC, with additional manipulations of review length (20 vs 40 words) and experience valence (positive, negative, control). Study 5 analyzes 70,027 tweets tied to 32 trending pop-culture hashtags (27,671 PC and 42,356 smartphone).
main_findings: Across datasets, content generated on smartphones contains a higher proportion of emotional language than content generated on PCs.

Smartphone-generated content is briefer and less specific, and brevity significantly mediates the device effect on emotionality; when length is experimentally held constant, device differences in emotionality attenuate.

The effect is stronger and easier to detect for positive affect, while negative affect shows weaker or less consistent detectability; results are robust to controls for temporal markers and to within-user analyses that reduce self-selection concerns.
one_line_summary: Across field and experimental evidence, the paper finds that writing on smartphones versus PCs leads to shorter, less specific, and more emotional user-generated content because brevity shifts writers toward gist-based expression.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior work on mobile word-of-mouth by moving beyond correlational evidence of higher emotion in mobile reviews to experimental tests, broader contexts, and mechanism evidence. It argues that the key driver is brevity-induced gist focus rather than real-time posting timing.
research_gap: Prior work had not clearly established whether smartphones fundamentally change what consumers write in UGC, nor had it provided strong causal evidence for why mobile-generated content appears more emotional. Existing explanations emphasizing real-time posting left the underlying mechanism unresolved.
research_question: Do consumers generate systematically different user-generated content on smartphones versus PCs, especially in emotionality and specificity? If so, is the difference caused by smartphone-induced brevity that pushes writers toward gist, rather than by timing of writing or user self-selection?
